entries.prompt:
Three-panel scientific illustration, 16:9, white background. Style: clean scientific illustration in the style of a textbook figure or Nature publication graphical abstract — flat colors, thin black outlines, professional academic aesthetic. Color palette: #007DC3 (blue), #5E9732 (green), #F78E1E (orange).

Panel 1 — left third, labeled 'NB03+NB05 Convergence': Two convergence arrow streams — one blue (#007DC3) labeled 'NB03 late-ChvI cohort' and one green (#5E9732) labeled 'NB05 H4 PG set' — both pointing to a central oval node labeled 'Pal CCNA_00784'. Below the node, two horizontal evidence bars labeled 'transcript +2.08 logFC' (blue) and 'protein +2.84 logFC' (orange). A star icon beside the node marks it as 'sole dual-modality hit'.

Panel 2 — center third, labeled 'Retrograde PL Transport': Membrane cross-section schematic. Bottom layer labeled 'IM', center space labeled 'periplasm', top layer labeled 'OM outer leaflet: phospholipid (no LPS)'. Tol-Pal complex shown as a transmembrane protein assembly: TolA (IM-spanning rod), TolB (periplasm disk), Pal (OM-anchored anchor). A curved orange arrow runs retrograde from OM outer leaflet downward to IM labeled 'retrograde PL transport'. A small ghost schematic crossed out labeled 'old model: LPS anchoring'.

Panel 3 — right third, labeled 'Caulobacter OM Constriction': Caulobacter predivisional cell shown as an elongated oval with a visible midcell constriction ring. Tol-Pal complex indicated at the constriction ring with a bracket. Label reads 'OM constriction essential (Yeh 2010)'. A bold upward arrow on Pal reads 'Pal upregulated in Δlpxc'.

Panel header labels in clean sans-serif directly above each panel. Do not include any other text, captions, titles, or annotations.

A scientific illustration in the style of a Nature publication graphical abstract showing the outer membrane architecture of Gram-negative bacteria — split into two side-by-side labeled panels. LEFT PANEL (labeled 'Canonical Gram-negative OM'): a stylized cross-section of the outer membrane bilayer viewed from the side. The outer leaflet is decorated with LPS molecules — each showing a lipid A anchor with two branching fatty-acid chains embedded in the outer leaflet (shown in #F78E1E orange), connected to a short polysaccharide chain extending outward into the extracellular space. The inner leaflet shows rounded phospholipid headgroups. A thin undulating peptidoglycan mesh layer is visible just inside the inner membrane. The bilayer leaflets and membrane core are colored #007DC3 blue. The panel has a neutral border indicating a normal, intact cell. RIGHT PANEL (labeled 'Caulobacter crescentus ΔlpxC — viable'): the identical outer membrane cross-section view, but the outer leaflet is completely free of lipid A anchors and LPS chains — the outer leaflet is maintained by smaller, simpler anionic lipid headgroups without the characteristic branched lipid A tail. The outer membrane and cell are intact and structurally complete, with a subtle #5E9732 green border or small checkmark badge indicating viability. Between the two panels, a clean vertical dashed divider line with a scissors icon and struck-through gene label 'lpxC' to indicate the genetic deletion event. A small tag below the right panel carries the text label '1 of 4 Gram-negatives to survive'. Style: clean scientific illustration, flat colors, thin black outlines, white background, professional academic aesthetic. Color palette: #007DC3 blue for membrane bilayer, #5E9732 green for viable Caulobacter state marker, #F78E1E orange for LPS lipid A anchor chains. Aspect ratio 16:9. Text labels 'Canonical Gram-negative OM', 'Caulobacter crescentus ΔlpxC — viable', '1 of 4 Gram-negatives to survive', 'lpxC' (strikethrough) in clean sans-serif, placed as described. Do not include any other text, captions, titles, or annotations beyond the named labels.

A scientific illustration with three side-by-side panels framing the three open research questions of a Caulobacter lipid A rescue study. Style: clean scientific illustration in the style of a textbook figure or Nature publication graphical abstract — flat colors, thin black outlines, professional academic aesthetic. KBase brand palette: #007DC3 (blue), #5E9732 (green), #F78E1E (orange). White background. Aspect ratio 16:9. Panel 1 (labeled 'Q1: Fur regulon'): A Fur repressor protein (labeled 'Fur') releasing downstream gene icons from repression, divided into two question groups — 'operationally required?' (#5E9732 green; icons: TBDT barrel and envelope transport channel) and 'bystanders?' (gray; icon: respiratory-chain complex). A large question mark over the gene-sorting step. Panel 2 (labeled 'Q2: Sphingolipid + Lpt'): Cross-section of the Caulobacter Gram-negative envelope — inner membrane (labeled 'IM'), periplasm with peptidoglycan layer, outer membrane (labeled 'OM'). In the OM outer leaflet: lipid A molecules marked with a gray X (absent/deleted), replaced by sphingolipid molecules with distinct polar head groups (#F78E1E orange), labeled 'constitutive pool'. A schematic Lpt transport complex (labeled 'Lpt apparatus') bridges IM to OM; a question arrow above it reads 'repurposed for sphingolipid transport?' Panel 3 (labeled 'Q3: Species specificity'): Four bacterial cell outlines in a 2x2 grid. Caulobacter crescentus (#5E9732 green outline) contains two small pathway icons labeled 'spt/cerR' and 'ChvG-ChvI'. Three comparator outlines (#007DC3 blue; labeled 'A. baumannii', 'N. meningitidis', 'M. catarrhalis') display the same pathway icons each marked with a gray X (structurally absent). A bracket label reads 'Why structurally unavailable?' Text labels: 'Q1: Fur regulon', 'Q2: Sphingolipid + Lpt', 'Q3: Species specificity', 'Fur', 'operationally required?', 'bystanders?', 'IM', 'OM', 'Lpt apparatus', 'constitutive pool', 'repurposed for sphingolipid transport?', 'spt/cerR', 'ChvG-ChvI', 'Caulobacter crescentus', 'A. baumannii', 'N. meningitidis', 'M. catarrhalis', 'Why structurally unavailable?' All labels in clean sans-serif. Do not include any other text, captions, titles, or annotations.
